title: Talk Freely, Execute Strictly: Schema-Gated Agentic AI for Flexible and Reproducible Scientific Workflows
author: Strickland, Joel, Vijeta, Arjun, Moores, Chris, Bodek, Oliwia, Nenchev, Bogdan, Whitehead, Thomas, Phillips, Charles, Tassenberg, Karl, Conduit, Gareth, Pellegrini, Ben
summary: This paper addresses the fundamental tension between conversational flexibility and deterministic execution in LLM-powered scientific workflows. Through interviews with 18 experts across 10 industrial R&D organizations, the authors identify competing requirements: users want natural language interaction without workflow rigidity, yet science demands reproducibility and governance. They propose schema-gated orchestration, where a machine-checkable specification becomes a mandatory execution boundary, ensuring nothing runs unless validated. The paper evaluates 20 existing systems across five architectural groups using a novel multi-model LLM scoring protocol (15 sessions, 3 LLM families) achieving high inter-rater agreement (Krippendorff's α=0.80–0.98), revealing an empirical Pareto front but identifying a convergence zone. Three operational principles guide adoption: clarification-before-execution, constrained plan-act orchestration, and tool-to-workflow-level gating.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2603.06394 (cs) [Submitted on 6 Mar 2026] Title: Talk Freely, Execute Strictly: Schema-Gated Agentic AI for Flexible and Reproducible Scientific Workflows Authors: Joel Strickland , Arjun Vijeta , Chris Moores , Oliwia Bodek , Bogdan Nenchev , Thomas Whitehead , Charles Phillips , Karl Tassenberg , Gareth Conduit , Ben Pellegrini View a PDF of the paper titled Talk Freely, Execute Strictly: Schema-Gated Agentic AI for Flexible and Reproducible Scientific Workflows, by Joel Strickland and 9 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can now translate a researcher's plain-language goal into executable computation, yet scientific workflows demand determinism, provenance, and governance that are difficult to guarantee when an LLM decides what runs. Semi-structured interviews with 18 experts across 10 industrial R&D stakeholders surface 2 competing requirements--deterministic, constrained execution and conversational flexibility without workflow rigidity--together with boundary properties (human-in-the-loop control and transparency) that any resolution must satisfy. We propose schema-gated orchestration as the resolving principle: the schema becomes a mandatory execution boundary at the composed-workflow level, so that nothing runs unless the complete action--including cross-step dependencies--validates against a machine-checkable specification. We operationalize the 2 requirements as execution determinism (ED) and conversational flexibility (CF), and use these axes to review 20 systems spanning 5 architectural groups along a validation-scope spectrum. Scores are assigned via a multi-model protocol--15 independent sessions across 3 LLM families--yielding substantial-to-near-perfect inter-model agreement (Krippendorff a=0.80 for ED and a=0.98 for CF), demonstrating that multi-model LLM scoring can serve as a reusable alternative to human expert panels for architectural assessment. The resulting landscape reveals an empirical Pareto front--no reviewed system achieves both high flexibility and high determinism--but a convergence zone emerges between the generative and workflow-centric extremes. We argue that a schema-gated architecture, separating conversational from execution authority, is positioned to decouple this trade-off, and distill 3 operational principles--clarification-before-execution, constrained plan-act orchestration, and tool-to-workflow-level gating--to guide adoption. takeaways: Schema-gated orchestration decouples conversational flexibility from execution determinism by separating human communication authority from machine execution authority.
Multi-model LLM scoring (Krippendorff's α≥0.80) is a reusable, scalable alternative to expert panels for evaluating architectural trade-offs.
No existing system achieves both high conversational flexibility and high execution determinism; convergence emerges between generative and workflow-centric extremes.
Three design principles resolve the trade-off: clarification-before-execution, constrained plan-act orchestration, and workflow-level gating.
Empirical validation via 18 expert stakeholders across industrial R&D confirms human-in-the-loop control and transparency are non-negotiable boundary properties.

title: Cost-Aware Model Orchestration for LLM-based Systems
author: Smirnova, Daria, Nasiri, Hamid, Adamska, Marta, Yu, Zhengxin, Garraghan, Peter
summary: This research paper addresses a critical limitation in LLM-based model orchestration systems: existing orchestrators rely on qualitative model descriptions for decision-making, leading to suboptimal model selection, reduced accuracy, and increased costs. The authors propose a cost-aware model selection method that incorporates quantitative performance characteristics and explicit performance-cost trade-offs into orchestration decisions. Experimental results demonstrate substantial improvements across multiple dimensions, including 0.90%-11.92% accuracy gains, up to 54% energy efficiency improvements, and dramatic latency reduction from 4.51 seconds to 7.2 milliseconds in model selection. This work is relevant for practitioners building production LLM systems where cost efficiency and task accuracy directly impact operational expenses and user experience.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2512.01099 (cs) [Submitted on 30 Nov 2025 ( v1 ), last revised 17 Apr 2026 (this version, v2)] Title: Cost-Aware Model Orchestration for LLM-based Systems Authors: Daria Smirnova , Hamid Nasiri , Marta Adamska , Zhengxin Yu , Peter Garraghan View a PDF of the paper titled Cost-Aware Model Orchestration for LLM-based Systems, by Daria Smirnova and 4 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: As modern artificial intelligence (AI) systems become more advanced and capable, they can leverage a wide range of tools and models to perform complex tasks. The task of orchestrating these models is increasingly performed by Large Language Models (LLMs) that rely on qualitative descriptions of models for decision-making. However, the descriptions provided to existing LLM-based orchestrators frequently do not reflect true model capabilities and performance characteristics, leading to suboptimal model selection, reduced task accuracy, and increased cost. In this paper, we conduct an empirical analysis of LLM-based orchestration limitations and propose a cost-aware model selection method that accounts for performance-cost trade-offs by incorporating quantitative model performance characteristics within decision-making. Initial experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method increases accuracy by 0.90%-11.92% across various evaluated tasks, achieves up to a 54% energy efficiency improvement, and reduces orchestrator model selection latency from 4.51 s to 7.2 ms. Comments: 9 pages, 5 figures. Accepted at EuroMLSys '26, Edinburgh, Scotland UK Subjects: Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI) Cite as: arXiv:2512.01099 [cs.AI] (or arXiv:2512.01099v2 [cs.AI] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2512.01099 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Daria Smirnova [ view email ] [v1] Sun, 30 Nov 2025 21:46:54 UTC (485 KB) [v2] Fri, 17 Apr 2026 13:11:16 UTC (1,166 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled Cost-Aware Model Orchestration for LLM-based Systems, by Daria Smirnova and 4 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.AI < prev | next > new | recent | 2025-12 Change to browse by: cs References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: LLM orchestrators using only qualitative model descriptions make suboptimal selections; quantitative performance metrics improve accuracy 0.90%-11.92%.
Cost-aware orchestration achieves up to 54% energy efficiency gains by explicitly modeling performance-cost trade-offs.
Model selection latency reduced 626× (4.51s to 7.2ms) via improved orchestration method, critical for real-time systems.
Quantitative characteristics enable better performance prediction than natural language descriptions alone.

title: Claude Code Expertise
summary: This article examines Claude's code generation and analysis capabilities within the context of agentic AI design patterns. It discusses how Claude can be leveraged as a component in autonomous agent systems, focusing on its strengths in understanding, writing, and reasoning about code. The piece is relevant for developers building AI-assisted development tools or autonomous coding agents, highlighting practical applications where Claude's language understanding translates to effective code manipulation and generation.
takeaways: Claude excels at code generation, analysis, and debugging tasks within agentic workflows
Code expertise is a key component when designing AI agents for software development
Claude's reasoning capabilities enable effective handling of complex code refactoring and architecture decisions
Integration of code understanding with agent patterns improves autonomous software engineering systems

Without actual article body, methodology, or arguments to evaluate, this cannot be meaningfully summarized. A technical reader cannot extract concrete insights, findings, or recommendations from this stub.

title: Meeting SLOs, Slashing Hours: Automated Enterprise LLM Optimization with OptiKIT
author: Santavas, Nicholas, Eissa, Kareem, Cieplicka, Patrycja, Florek, Piotr, Nulli, Matteo, Vasilev, Stefan, Hashemi, Seyyed Hadi, Gasteratos, Antonios, Khadivi, Shahram
summary: OptiKIT is a distributed LLM optimization framework that automates model compression and tuning workflows to democratize enterprise LLM deployment. The system addresses the critical challenge of scaling AI initiatives within constrained GPU budgets by enabling non-expert teams to optimize models without deep specialized knowledge. OptiKIT provides dynamic resource allocation, staged pipeline execution with automatic cleanup, and seamless enterprise integration, delivering over 2x GPU throughput improvement in production while reducing optimization complexity and cost.
content: Computer Science > Distributed, Parallel, and Cluster Computing arXiv:2601.20408 (cs) [Submitted on 28 Jan 2026 ( v1 ), last revised 8 Jun 2026 (this version, v2)] Title: Meeting SLOs, Slashing Hours: Automated Enterprise LLM Optimization with OptiKIT Authors: Nicholas Santavas , Kareem Eissa , Patrycja Cieplicka , Piotr Florek , Matteo Nulli , Stefan Vasilev , Seyyed Hadi Hashemi , Antonios Gasteratos , Shahram Khadivi View a PDF of the paper titled Meeting SLOs, Slashing Hours: Automated Enterprise LLM Optimization with OptiKIT, by Nicholas Santavas and 8 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Enterprise LLM deployment faces a critical scalability challenge: organizations must optimize models systematically to scale AI initiatives within constrained compute budgets, yet the specialized expertise required for manual optimization remains a niche and scarce skillset. This challenge is particularly evident in managing GPU utilization across heterogeneous infrastructure while enabling teams with diverse workloads and limited LLM optimization experience to deploy models efficiently. We present OPTIKIT, a distributed LLM optimization framework that democratizes model compression and tuning by automating complex optimization workflows for non-expert teams. OPTIKIT provides dynamic resource allocation, staged pipeline execution with automatic cleanup, and seamless enterprise integration. In production, it delivers more than 2x GPU throughput improvement while empowering application teams to achieve consistent performance improvements without deep LLM optimization expertise. We share both the platform design and key engineering insights into resource management, pipeline orchestration, and integration patterns that enable large-scale, production-grade democratization of model optimization. Finally, we open-source the system to enable external contributions and broader reproducibility. System features dynamic resource allocation, staged pipelines with cleanup, and enterprise integration patterns for production-grade model optimization
Democratizes model compression and tuning to enable diverse teams with limited LLM expertise to deploy efficiently within constrained budgets
Open-sourced framework balances resource management, pipeline orchestration, and integration patterns for large-scale reproducibility

title: MARCO: Multi-Agent Real-time Chat Orchestration
author: Shrimal, Anubhav, Kanagaraj, Stanley, Biswas, Kriti, Raghuraman, Swarnalatha, Nediyanchath, Anish, Zhang, Yi, Yenigalla, Promod
summary: MARCO is a multi-agent orchestration framework for automating complex tasks using large language models in real-time chat environments. The framework addresses critical challenges in LLM-based systems including inconsistent output formatting, hallucination of functions and parameters, and limited domain knowledge by implementing robust guardrails, output validation, and error recovery mechanisms. Evaluated on restaurant service and retail conversation datasets, MARCO achieves 94.48% and 92.74% task execution accuracy respectively, while reducing latency by 44.91% and costs by 33.71%. The modular design enables adaptation across domains and supports multi-turn interactions, making it relevant for practitioners building production LLM systems that require reliability and cost efficiency.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2410.21784 (cs) [Submitted on 29 Oct 2024] Title: MARCO: Multi-Agent Real-time Chat Orchestration Authors: Anubhav Shrimal , Stanley Kanagaraj , Kriti Biswas , Swarnalatha Raghuraman , Anish Nediyanchath , Yi Zhang , Promod Yenigalla View a PDF of the paper titled MARCO: Multi-Agent Real-time Chat Orchestration, by Anubhav Shrimal and 6 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Large language model advancements have enabled the development of multi-agent frameworks to tackle complex, real-world problems such as to automate tasks that require interactions with diverse tools, reasoning, and human collaboration. We present MARCO, a Multi-Agent Real-time Chat Orchestration framework for automating tasks using LLMs. MARCO addresses key challenges in utilizing LLMs for complex, multi-step task execution. It incorporates robust guardrails to steer LLM behavior, validate outputs, and recover from errors that stem from inconsistent output formatting, function and parameter hallucination, and lack of domain knowledge. Through extensive experiments we demonstrate MARCO's superior performance with 94.48% and 92.74% accuracy on task execution for Digital Restaurant Service Platform conversations and Retail conversations datasets respectively along with 44.91% improved latency and 33.71% cost reduction. We also report effects of guardrails in performance gain along with comparisons of various LLM models, both open-source and proprietary. The modular and generic design of MARCO allows it to be adapted for automating tasks across domains and to execute complex usecases through multi-turn interactions. Comments: EMNLP 2024 Industry Track Subjects: Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI) ; Computation and Language (cs.CL); Machine Learning (cs.LG); Multiagent Systems (cs.MA) Cite as: arXiv:2410.21784 [cs.AI] (or arXiv:2410.21784v1 [cs.AI] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2410.21784 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Anubhav Shrimal [ view email ] [v1] Tue, 29 Oct 2024 06:42:27 UTC (3,512 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled MARCO: Multi-Agent Real-time Chat Orchestration, by Anubhav Shrimal and 6 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.AI < prev | next > new | recent | 2024-10 Change to browse by: cs cs.CL cs.LG cs.MA References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... Modular design supports cross-domain adaptation without requiring domain-specific retraining
Systematic comparison of open-source and proprietary LLMs guides model selection
Multi-turn conversation support enables complex, multi-step task automation

title: AI Agents and Agentic AI-Navigating a Plethora of Concepts for Future Manufacturing
author: Ren, Yinwang, Liu, Yangyang, Ji, Tang, Xu, Xun
summary: This arXiv research paper systematically reviews AI agents and agentic AI paradigms, examining their evolution and technological foundations. The authors clarify definitions and capability boundaries of LLM-based agents, multimodal LLM-based agents, and agentic AI systems, then explore their applications and integration into smart manufacturing. The paper addresses a critical gap in literature by providing structured analysis of how generative AI advances—particularly in semantic comprehension, complex reasoning, and autonomous decision-making—can enhance manufacturing systems. This work is relevant for researchers and practitioners developing autonomous systems in industrial settings.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2507.01376 (cs) [Submitted on 2 Jul 2025] Title: AI Agents and Agentic AI-Navigating a Plethora of Concepts for Future Manufacturing Authors: Yinwang Ren , Yangyang Liu , Tang Ji , Xun Xu View a PDF of the paper titled AI Agents and Agentic AI-Navigating a Plethora of Concepts for Future Manufacturing, by Yinwang Ren and 3 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: AI agents are autonomous systems designed to perceive, reason, and act within dynamic environments. With the rapid advancements in generative AI (GenAI), large language models (LLMs) and multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have significantly improved AI agents' capabilities in semantic comprehension, complex reasoning, and autonomous decision-making. At the same time, the rise of Agentic AI highlights adaptability and goal-directed autonomy in dynamic and complex environments. LLMs-based AI Agents (LLM-Agents), MLLMs-based AI Agents (MLLM-Agents), and Agentic AI contribute to expanding AI's capabilities in information processing, environmental perception, and autonomous decision-making, opening new avenues for smart manufacturing. However, the definitions, capability boundaries, and practical applications of these emerging AI paradigms in smart manufacturing remain unclear. To address this gap, this study systematically reviews the evolution of AI and AI agent technologies, examines the core concepts and technological advancements of LLM-Agents, MLLM-Agents, and Agentic AI, and explores their potential applications in and integration into manufacturing, along with the potential challenges they may face. Agentic AI emphasizes adaptability and goal-directed autonomy in complex manufacturing scenarios
Paper clarifies conceptual boundaries between LLM-Agents, MLLM-Agents, and Agentic AI for manufacturing applications
Identifies challenges for deploying these AI paradigms in smart manufacturing contexts

title: SAGE: Tool-Augmented LLM Task Solving Strategies in Scalable Multi-Agent Environments
author: Strehlow, Robert K., Küster, Tobias, Kupke, Oskar F., Kurps, Brandon Llanque, Sivrikaya, Fikret, Albayrak, Sahin
summary: SAGE is a conversational AI framework for integrating tools with Large Language Models in multi-agent environments. Built on the OPACA framework, it addresses the challenge of dynamically discovering and executing custom tools without manual pre-definition. The system implements multiple task-solving strategies using different prompting methods and agentic approaches, evaluated against benchmark services. The work demonstrates that different strategies exhibit distinct strengths and weaknesses, and provides open-source implementations to enable reproducible research in tool-augmented LLM task solving at scale.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2601.09750 (cs) [Submitted on 12 Jan 2026] Title: SAGE: Tool-Augmented LLM Task Solving Strategies in Scalable Multi-Agent Environments Authors: Robert K. Strehlow , Tobias Küster , Oskar F. Kupke , Brandon Llanque Kurps , Fikret Sivrikaya , Sahin Albayrak View a PDF of the paper titled SAGE: Tool-Augmented LLM Task Solving Strategies in Scalable Multi-Agent Environments, by Robert K. Strehlow and 5 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have proven to work well in question-answering scenarios, but real-world applications often require access to tools for live information or actuation. For this, LLMs can be extended with tools, which are often defined in advance, also allowing for some fine-tuning for specific use cases. However, rapidly evolving software landscapes and individual services require the constant development and integration of new tools. Domain- or company-specific tools can greatly elevate the usefulness of an LLM, but such custom tools can be problematic to integrate, or the LLM may fail to reliably understand and use them. For this, we need strategies to define new tools and integrate them into the LLM dynamically, as well as robust and scalable zero-shot prompting methods that can make use of those tools in an efficient manner. In this paper, we present SAGE, a specialized conversational AI interface, based on the OPACA framework for tool discovery and execution. The integration with OPACA makes it easy to add new tools or services for the LLM to use, while SAGE itself presents rich extensibility and modularity. This not only provides the ability to seamlessly switch between different models (e.g. GPT, LLAMA), but also to add and select prompting methods, involving various setups of differently prompted agents for selecting and executing tools and evaluating the results. We implemented a number of task-solving strategies, making use of agentic concepts and prompting methods in various degrees of complexity, and evaluated those against a comprehensive set of benchmark services. The results are promising and highlight the distinct strengths and weaknesses of different task-solving strategies. Both SAGE and the OPACA framework, as well as the different benchmark services and results, are available as Open Source/Open Data on GitHub. takeaways: Dynamic tool integration via OPACA framework eliminates need for manual pre-definition of custom tools
Multiple prompting strategies with varying agent complexity show different performance tradeoffs on benchmarks
System supports seamless model switching (GPT, LLAMA) and extensible agent selection mechanisms
Zero-shot prompting methods enable robust tool use without task-specific fine-tuning
Open-source release includes SAGE, OPACA, benchmarks, and results for reproducibility

title: LLM-Powered AI Agent Systems and Their Applications in Industry
author: Liang, Guannan, Tong, Qianqian
summary: This research paper examines the evolution and current state of LLM-powered agent systems, contrasting them with traditional rule-based approaches. The authors categorize modern agents into software-based, physical, and adaptive hybrid systems, demonstrating applications across customer service, software development, manufacturing, education, finance, and healthcare. The paper identifies critical challenges—inference latency, output uncertainty, evaluation metrics, and security vulnerabilities—and proposes mitigation strategies. It provides a comprehensive overview of how multi-modal LLMs enable agents to process diverse data types (text, images, audio, structured data) for more flexible and adaptive real-world deployment.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2505.16120 (cs) [Submitted on 22 May 2025 ( v1 ), last revised 12 Jun 2026 (this version, v3)] Title: LLM-Powered AI Agent Systems and Their Applications in Industry Authors: Guannan Liang , Qianqian Tong View a PDF of the paper titled LLM-Powered AI Agent Systems and Their Applications in Industry, by Guannan Liang and Qianqian Tong View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: The emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs) has reshaped agent systems. Unlike traditional rule-based agents with limited task scope, LLM-powered agents offer greater flexibility, cross-domain reasoning, and natural language interaction. Moreover, with the integration of multi-modal LLMs, current agent systems are highly capable of processing diverse data modalities, including text, images, audio, and structured tabular data, enabling richer and more adaptive real-world behavior. This paper comprehensively examines the evolution of agent systems from the pre-LLM era to current LLM-powered architectures. We categorize agent systems into software-based, physical, and adaptive hybrid systems, highlighting applications across customer service, software development, manufacturing automation, personalized education, financial trading, and healthcare. We further discuss the primary challenges posed by LLM-powered agents, including high inference latency, output uncertainty, lack of evaluation metrics, and security vulnerabilities, and propose potential solutions to mitigate these concerns. Comments: This is the author's accepted version of the paper accepted to appear at IEEE AIIoT 2025. The final version will be available via IEEE Xplore. \c{opyright}2025 IEEE. takeaways: LLM agents surpass rule-based systems through cross-domain reasoning, natural language interaction, and multi-modal processing capabilities
Six major industrial applications span customer service, software development, manufacturing, education, financial trading, and healthcare domains
Key challenges include high inference latency, output uncertainty, insufficient evaluation metrics, and security vulnerabilities requiring targeted solutions
Multi-modal LLM integration enables richer real-world behavior by processing text, images, audio, and structured tabular data simultaneously
Agent systems evolve from pre-LLM rule-based architectures to flexible LLM-powered hybrid systems with adaptive capabilities
